title: Arroz con Leche (Spanish Rice Pudding)

ingredients.name: short-grain or regular white rice
visual_checks: rinse well; short-grain gives the creamiest result
ingredients.notes: rinse well; short-grain gives the creamiest result

subs.name: short-grain sona masoori rice or even basmati works well; cooking time stays the same
a mix of 300 ml full-fat coconut milk and 500 ml regular milk for a tropical note
4 tbsp condensed milk stirred in at the end — richly sweet and thickens the pudding further
1 pod of green cardamom (lightly crushed) for an Indian-spiced version; remove before serving

ingredients.name: lemon (zest only, in strips)
visual_checks: use a vegetable peeler; avoid the white pith
ingredients.notes: use a vegetable peeler; avoid the white pith

subs.name: 1 pod of green cardamom (lightly crushed) for an Indian-spiced version; remove before serving

steps.text: Rinse the rice in cold water until the water runs nearly clear. Drain well.

Pour the milk into a heavy-bottomed saucepan. Add the cinnamon stick, lemon zest strips, and a pinch of salt. Place on medium heat and bring to a gentle simmer, stirring occasionally to prevent the milk from forming a skin or scorching on the base.

Add the rinsed rice to the simmering milk. Reduce the flame to its lowest setting and cook uncovered, stirring every 3–4 minutes with a wooden spoon to prevent sticking, for 35–40 minutes until the rice is very soft and the mixture has thickened to a loose, creamy porridge-like consistency.

Add the sugar and stir until fully dissolved. Taste and add more sugar if desired. The pudding will thicken considerably as it cools, so remove from heat while it still looks a little looser than you want it.
Remove and discard the cinnamon stick and lemon zest strips. Stir in vanilla extract if using.
Spoon into individual bowls or glasses. Serve warm with a dusting of ground cinnamon on top, or press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate for 2 hours for a chilled version.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: milk → a mix of 300 ml full-fat coconut milk and 500 ml regular milk for a tropical note; sugar → 4 tbsp condensed milk stirred in at the end — richly sweet and thickens the pudding further; cinnamon → 1 pod of green cardamom (lightly crushed) for an Indian-spiced version; remove before serving; rice → short-grain sona masoori rice or even basmati works well; cooking time stays the same
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is essentially a relative of kheer or payasam — use the same technique on a kadai over the lowest flame, stirring regularly. Full-fat cow or buffalo milk gives the richest result. Sona Masoori or ponni rice works perfectly. Serve chilled in small steel bowls dusted with cinnamon — it will feel pleasantly familiar to anyone who has eaten rice kheer.
